coredata.dc:description: Graphene, a unique 2D honeycomb structure consisting of conjugated sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with fascinating properties such as large theoretical surface area (2630m2/g), better thermal conductivity (5000W/m per K), special electronic conductivity (200,000cm2/V per s), higher biocompatibility, low cost for scalable production, and ease of surface functionalization has been both conveniently and conventionally used to develop novel electronic materials including transparent conductors and ultrafast transistors. Recent understanding of various chemical properties of graphene however has facilitated its application range, which is now expanding beyond electronic and chemical applications toward biomedical areas, such as precise biosensing through graphene-quenched fluorescence, graphene-enhanced cell differentiation and growth, etc. Nanosized graphene derivatives are now finding regular use as in vivo/in vitro imaging agents and portable carriers for the targeted delivery of various anticancer drugs. Further research involving the functionalization and application of inorganic nanoparticles onto graphene has also gained considerable impetus. This chapter deals with a brief discussion on the historical backgrounds, physicochemical properties, synthetic methodologies, and potential use and application of graphene and its derivatives in the field of biomedicine, with special focus on the drug/gene delivery, imaging, therapeutic, and biosensing applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene, a unique 2D honeycomb structure consisting of conjugated sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with fascinating properties such as large theoretical surface area (2630m2/g), better thermal conductivity (5000W/m per K), special electronic conductivity (200,000cm2/V per s), higher biocompatibility, low cost for scalable production, and ease of surface functionalization has been both conveniently and conventionally used to develop novel electronic materials including transparent conductors and ultrafast transistors. Recent understanding of various chemical properties of graphene however has facilitated its application range, which is now expanding beyond electronic and chemical applications toward biomedical areas, such as precise biosensing through graphene-quenched fluorescence, graphene-enhanced cell differentiation and growth, etc. Nanosized graphene derivatives are now finding regular use as in vivo/in vitro imaging agents and portable carriers for the targeted delivery of various anticancer drugs. Further research involving the functionalization and application of inorganic nanoparticles onto graphene has also gained considerable impetus. This chapter deals with a brief discussion on the historical backgrounds, physicochemical properties, synthetic methodologies, and potential use and application of graphene and its derivatives in the field of biomedicine, with special focus on the drug/gene delivery, imaging, therapeutic, and biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Sir Richard Feynman’s famous lecture There Is Plenty of Room at the Bottom in 1959 attracted many researchers to work in the field of nanomaterials (Feynman, 1964). These are the materials having dimension in the range of 1–100nm, possessing unique physicochemical properties, different from that of their bulk counter parts (Fahlman, 2011; Kreibig and Vollmer, 1995). For last few decades, a variety of nanomaterials have been developed by scientists which have further played a quintessential role in the emergence as well as advancement of a number of technologies in the service of mankind (Chen et al., 2010a; Gratzel, 2001; Walter et al., 2010). Moreover, the field of nanotechnology is not confined to any particular branch of science a multidisciplinary field of study encompassing the basic sciences such as physics, chemistry, mathematics, biology and engineering.

Graphene is a two-dimensional (2D) single atom–thick carbon sheet containing the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in a honeycomb lattice-like framework. Graphene was discovered by Geim, for which he received the prestigious Nobel Prize in Physics in 2010. Physical attributes such as higher surface area, better electrical/thermal conductivity, and excellent optical features attracted extensive interest since from its discovery by Geim et al. in 2004 (Avouris, 2010). Because of its unique physical and chemical properties, graphene has been used in the field of energy storage/conversion and also has also been employed in designing of biosensors, biomarkers, platforms for targeted drug delivery, tissue engineering, etc. (Jiang, 2011; Mao et al., 2013a; Shen et al., 2012a; Yavari and Koratkar, 2012). Graphene also holds a better candidature for the biomedical platform owing to its intrinsic properties, simple molecular design and compatibility with other nanoparticles (Gao and Duan, 2015). Although other forms of carbon, such as the carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerene, and carbon fibers have been studied, very recently much attention of the scientific community has been focused on exploring and using the untapped potential of graphene in a multitude of biomedical applications. By taking graphene as a base material, a number of derivatives have been prepared, such as graphene oxide (GO), graphite oxide, thin layer graphene sheets, and graphene quantum dots or carbon quantum dots, etc. (Wang and Qiu, 2016; Yang et al., 2013b). It has been known that the single layer of graphene occupies 2630m2/g of specific surface area with superior electrical conductivity and mechanical strength. It makes possible to install an advanced foundation for DNA sequencing over the conventional biological nanopores (α-hemolysin and MspA) and designing of sensors for the detection of bioanalytes (glucose, hemoglobin, cholesterol, etc.) (Zhu et al., 2016). Furthermore, the development of graphene derivatives and their functionalization has facilitated exploring more applications in the targeted drug/gene delivery and tissue engineering areas (Nurunnabi et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2012a). Further functionalization on the GO surface opens up possibilities to append a number of biomolecules onto its surface. Moreover, in contact with an external stimulus (like pH, magnetic radiation, electromagnetic irradiations, etc.), the functionalized GO furnishes new opportunities for advanced drug delivery system (Pan et al., 2012).

In the following sections, a brief discussion on the historical background, synthesis, and properties of graphene and its derivatives have been presented, along with an overview on their importance in various biomedical applications; particularly development of biosensors, imaging, drug/gene delivery, etc. has been elaborately discussed.

Graphite (meaning writing stone), also known as black lead or plumbago, has been documented to be in use since the 4th century BC by the Marita culture as a paint to decorate the ceramic pottery in Southeastern Europe. Graphite has been mainly used for marking of instruments and as a solid lubricant. Later, it found use for writing purposes (graphite in pencils). Moreover, the excellent electrical and thermal conductivity of graphite has enabled it to be used as electrodes and heating elements in the blast furnace in industries (Delhaes, 2001; Enoki et al., 2003). The single sheet of graphite termed as graphene was first reported in 1987 as one of the constituents of graphite intercalated compounds. Although the study on graphene has been traced back to the prehistoric times, the use and research intensified only after 2004. In 2004 at Manchester University, Prof. Andre Geim and Prof. Konstantin Novoselov successfully separated the single layer of graphene from the graphite by scotch tape method and explored their various properties. Subsequently, scientists and technologists all over the globe are now actively pursuing graphene to unearth more information and employ it in various fields, ranging from energy to biomedical applications.

Graphene is the main building block of many of the allotropic forms of carbon. It can be easily obtained from the bulk graphite by simple mechanical exfoliation or following various chemical routes. It can be also derived from other allotropes of carbon, i.e., by unzipping of the CNTs, fullerene, etc. It was found that, at some time before 1565, a large deposit of graphite was ascertained near Grey Knots, from the hamlet of Seathwaite in Borrowdale parish, Cumbria, England. Naturally, the graphite occurs in metamorphic rocks as a result of the reduction of sedimentary carbon compounds at the time of the metamorphism process It also occurs in igneous rocks, in meteorites (with the troilite, cliftonite, and silicate minerals) and also in some of minerals such as quartz, calcite, tourmaline, and mica, etc. Based on the United States Geological Survey of 2012, the global production of the natural graphite was 1,100,000 tons and India was occupied the second position after China as the major exporter. Graphite has also been exported from Brazil (75kt), North Korea (30kt) and Canada (26kt), and is not mined in the United States (“Graphite Statistics and Information”. USGS. Retrieved 2009-09-09).

Carbon has the smallest atomic radius and molecular volume, in comparison to the other elements of Group 14 of the periodic table. Due to the unique electronic configuration, carbon can form multiple bonds (single, double, and triple bond) in a number of its compounds. Due to its catenation property, carbon forms many compounds by covalent bonding among themselves. Moreover, it exists in three dimensional (graphite, diamond), two dimensional (graphene), one dimensional (CNT), and zero dimensional (fullerene, CQDs/GQDs) allotropic forms (Grima et al., 2015; Iijima, 1991; Kroto et al., 1985; Zhang et al., 2012). These are the crystalline form of carbon, whereas the wood/animal charcoal, lamp black, coke, etc. are amorphous in nature. The X-ray diffraction study revealed that the amorphous carbon consists of fine crystalline structures, for which they are regarded as the microcrystalline carbons. Some of the allotropes are briefly discussed in the proceeding sections.

It is a 3D allotrope of carbon, containing a large number of one atom–thick graphene layers. Here, each of the carbon atoms are sp2-hybridized and bonded to three adjacent carbon atoms, forming the covalent bond. Whereas, the unhybridized orbital of each carbon atom overlaps with the orbital of the adjacent one, forming a pi-bond, thus a pi-electron cloud is developed above and below of each of the sp2 bonded layer. This is the platform for the free movement of the unpaired electron, permitting the ease of passage of electricity, making graphite a good electrical conductor. Here, the CC bond distance was found to be 0.142nm and the distance between each of the individual layer is 0.33nm (Abergel et al., 2010). Moreover, each of the layers is attached to other by means of the weak van der Waals force of attraction, for which these layers freely slides over each other (Fig. 13.1 ). This provides softness, greasiness, and lubricating nature of graphite.

Like graphite, the diamond is also a 3D allotrope of carbon. Instead of sp2 hybridization, each of the constituent carbon atoms are sp3 hybridized, forming a tetrahedral structure (Fig. 13.2 ). Each of the four sp3 hybridized carbon occupies the central position of the tetrahedron and bonded to the adjacent four carbon atoms present at the corners of the tetrahedron by covalent linkage. This leads to forming a big polymeric three-dimensional structure with CC bond distance of 0.154nm. Because of this strong covalent linkage, the diamond is very hard, having higher density with an unusual higher melting point of 3500°C.

It is a one atom–thick planar 2D arrangement of the sp2 carbon atoms in a honeycomb lattice structure (Fig. 13.3 ). Many of these single layers are stacked to form a three-dimensional graphite. Here, the CC bond distance is found to be 0.142nm (Yin et al., 2015).

Although it has been observed in an electron microscope in 1962, the focus of the researchers to explore its applicability was only after the groundbreaking discovery by Sir Prof. Andre Geim and Prof. Konstantin Novoselov in 2004. Afterwards, many of the scientific efforts have been devoted to the scalable synthesis and study the application of graphene. Owing to its intrinsic properties, extreme high specific surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, better thermal/optical properties, it became an area of focus for the application in emerging fields.
It is the unidimensional allotrope of carbon containing the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms such as in graphene and graphite.

It has been categorized as single and multiwalled nanotubes, based on the number of layers present (Fig. 13.4 ). The CNTs align themselves in a rope-like structure by the π–π interactions. In 1991, it was discovered by Iijima of NEC (Nippon Electric Company, Limited) (Iijima, 1991).

It is a zero-dimensional allotrope of carbon, in which the carbon atoms are arranged in football (soccer)-like shape (Fig. 13.4), hence the name buckyball. It was discovered by Richard Smalley, Robert Curl, James Heath, Sean O’Brien, and Harold Kroto at Rice University in 1985 (Kroto et al., 1985). Later, Buckminsterfullerene (C60) was attributed to it after a famous architectural modeler Richard Buckminster Fuller, due to its resemblance with his geodesic dome. Here, both the hexagonal and pentagonal carbon rings are present, arranged in a specific manner to form such a hollow sphere.

Traditionally, quantum dots referred to the transition metal oxides/sulfides, but after the work by Xu et al. (2004), the term quantum dot was assigned to these carbon materials. The carbon quantum dots are the tiny carbon particles having a lateral dimension of less than 10nm with a quasi-spherical shape. Other allotropes of carbon have a zero band-gap, but when the size is reduced to the quantum confinement a band gap then develops and shows the luminescence behavior (Samantara et al., 2015, 2016). Due to their exciting luminescence performance, tuneable size, ease of preparation and functionalization, they are used broadly in many of the biomedical applications.

The excellent chemical structure of graphene provides better electrical, optical, mechanical properties that germinate both the commercial and academic research interest of many of the researchers globally. After its successful separation from bulk graphite in 2004 by Geim and Novoselov, many of the researchers put their effort for the scalable production of high quality graphene through a cost effective manner (Allen et al., 2010; Geim and Novoselov, 2007). As a result a number of significant synthetic protocols have been developed; each associated with certain advantages and limitations (Fig. 13.5 ). Out of them, the exfoliation of bulk graphite and deposition of various organic molecules on specific substrate been broadly adopted (Novoselov et al., 2013; Raccichini et al., 2015).

The depositions of the organic molecules are divided into: (1) thermal chemical vapor deposition (CVD) techniques and (2) plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) techniques (Kumar et al., 2013; Mattevi et al., 2011). With certain variations, both the techniques use substrate for the deposition and precursor mixtures (H2, Ar and CH4) in a particular proportion and higher temperature conditions. The first report on CVD synthesis of planar few layer graphene was carried out by vaporizing camphor on Ni foils in 2008/2009 (Zhang et al., 2013). It was observed that the developed graphene was 35 layers thick. Although the use of CVD technique produced high quality graphene with controlled thickness, it was still associated with certain limitations, viz, substrate specificity (Si and Cu), controlled number of layers, and difficult growth on wafer size substrates, etc. Thereafter, many of the researchers put their interest toward modifying the synthetic strategy and used the CVD in conjugation with the plasma, called PECVD (Geng et al., 2007; Kumar et al., 2013). The PECVD technique allows the deposition of the precursors at lower temperature on a variety of substrates (Si wafer and Ni, W, Mo and some other metal sheets as substrates). However, improvement in this method is still needed to bring out better control over the layer thickness and large scale production.

This exfoliation process may be carried out by either using the physical or chemical techniques. Mechanical cleavage by the scotch tape method, ball milling method, ultrasonication of graphite, epitaxial growth on the silicon carbide substrate, reduction of CO2 gas or dry ice, etc. are physical methods of synthesis (Hernandez et al., 2008; Jeon et al., 2012; Novoselov et al., 2004; Sutter, 2009; Zhao et al., 2010). The mechanical cleavage process produces high quality and pure graphene, but the scalable production is difficult, restricting its commercialization. In cases of ball-milling and ultrasonication treatment, pure graphene can also be derived quantitatively; but, there is also a chance for the presence of un-exfoliated graphite leading a poor product yield (Cai et al., 2012; Paton et al., 2014; Raccichini et al., 2015). Conversely the chemical-based synthetic protocols, such as unzipping CNTs, liquid exfoliation, chemical oxidation and reduction methods, etc. arouses much attention for their scalable and lower production costs (Choi et al., 2010; Chua and Pumera, 2014; Jiao et al., 2009). By unzipping CNTs, graphene can be derived, but the controlled reaction condition and cost of CNTs limits bulk production. In the case of liquid exfoliation method, the pristine graphite powders are dispersed in a solvent to weaken the van der Waals force of attraction between the individual layers (Cai et al., 2012; Raccichini et al., 2015). After that, on applying external forces such as ultrasonication, electric field or shearing force, the individual graphene sheets have been exfoliated (Cai et al., 2012; Paton et al., 2014). Although in this process some unexfoliated graphite powders may persist, the low cost and higher scalability make this process suitable for large scale production of graphene.

Conversely, the chemical oxidation and reduction process have been adopted as the prominent methods for the quantitative production of graphene. It is a two-step method; the first step involves the oxidation of graphite to graphene oxide and then reduction of the graphene oxide to form reduced graphene oxide (rGO; Chua and Pumera, 2014). Graphene oxide is a most defective form of graphene that can be produced by the chemical/electrochemical oxidation of graphite (Hummers and Offeman, 1958; Kovtyukhova et al., 1999). The chemical oxidation process involves the treatment of the graphite with the strong oxidizing agents following Hummer’s method, Brodie method, etc. (Eda and Chhowalla, 2010). Among them, the advanced form of Hummer’s method has been adopted broadly for the large scale synthesis of graphene oxide (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). Conversely, the reduction of graphene oxide can be carried out by treating it with reducing agents (hydrazine, sodium borohydride, potassium borohydride, lithium aluminum hydride, etc.), by high temperature annealing, or by following the hydrothermal/solvothermal processes (Dreyer et al., 2010; Park and Ruoff, 2009). Most of the reducing agents are very toxic and some of them are explosive, restricting handling in the aqueous medium-based reaction. Therefore, many attempts have been made to develop the nontoxic reduction strategies; including bacterial respiration, baker’s yeast treatment, use of Escherichia coli biomass, glucose, polyphenols, melatonin, potassium hydroxide, etc. as the reducing agents (Akhavan et al., 2012; Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2012). Although the product quality of the prepared rGO is low (due to the intrinsic and extrinsic defects in rGO), but the cost effectiveness, quantitative production, and simple procedure, make the oxidation-reduction process more viable for the commercial scale preparation of rGO.

Graphene is a 2D carbon material containing sp2 hybridized carbon arranged in a honeycomb lattice-like structure. Each of the three sp2 hybridized orbital is overlapped with the orbitals of adjacent three carbon atoms by means of covalent linkages. Whereas, the unhybridized orbital of each of the carbon atom is overlapped to form a π-bond leading to develop a π-electron cloud above and below the sp2 carbon network (Yang et al., 2013b). It provides a promising platform to conjugate biomolecules and fluorophores through the noncovalent linkage for the sensitive and selective detection of bioanalytes. The strong covalent linkage provide more mechanical strength (Young’s modulus of 1060GPa with a breaking strength of ~40N/m) to graphene, for which it has been recognized as an ideal support material for the cell culture growth (Lee et al., 2008). Moreover the excellent electrical conductivity and size-dependent electronic properties make graphene a promising material for biosensor and an effective fluorescent quencher for the optical detection of different biomolecules (Tombros et al., 2007). The large specific surface (2630m2/g) of the graphene enable to accommodate more of the biomolecules to promote the targeted drug delivery system and more therapeutic applications (Chae et al., 2004).

The existence of the π-electron cloud and developed structural defects in the 2D graphene/derivatives are the primary source of their luminescent behavior (Cao et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2011). Graphene oxide, which is the oxidized form of graphene, is generally obtained by chemical/electrochemical oxidation of graphite. Subsequently, the prepared GO are converted to rGO via chemical, thermal, or photochemical reduction processes. Since most of the chemical methods are followed for these conversions, some of the oxygen functionalities and defects remain in the GO and rGO surfaces unintentionally. Because of these functionalities the GO confines the π-electrons within the sp2 nanodomains, demonstrating the luminescence performance in the ultraviolet to near infrared region (NIR; Mao et al., 2013a). Besides the luminescence properties, the GO and rGO-based materials acts as the nanoquencher in fluorescence biosensors based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism (Kim et al., 2010; Morales-Narváez and Merkoçi, 2012). The large oxygen functional groups on the surface of the GO act as the holders to conjugate with various recognition molecules and functional materials to act as an efficient biomarker. These advanced properties of graphene and its emergent derivatives, GO/rGO, make them suitable material for the biosensing, bioimaging and many of the therapeutic applications that are discussed in the following sections. The size-reduced derivative of graphene is the graphene quantum dot or the carbon quantum dots have gathered so much attention because of their emerging salient properties. These are the highly oxidized tiny carbon particles containing both the sp2 carbon cluster and many of the oxygen functionalities on its surface (Hong et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2012c). Because of their tuneable optical properties, ease of size controlled preparation, negligible toxicity, nonblinking nature, and strong resistance to photobleaching, they are employed in various field of biomedical applications (Lin et al., 2014).

The surface composition and the lateral dimension of the graphene have a significant contribution toward its biomedical applications. However the zero band gap and fluorescence quenching properties, makes impossible to use for cell imaging and labeling purpose (Cornelissen et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2013a). Therefore, the surface engineering of graphene became an essential tool to make it a suitable probe for the multimodal biomedical applications. It has been observed that, the pristine graphene (containing only sp2 hybridized carbon network) and the rGO (fewer number of functional groups present) are more toxic, compared to the graphene oxide (with numerous oxygenated functionalities) (Schinwald et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2016). Therefore the surface engineering of the graphene arouses much attention to improve the bioanalytical properties (like enhanced biocompatibility, drug loading efficiency and increased specificity/sensitivity enabling targeted delivery), making a suitable platform for biomedical applications. Conversely, the graphite (graphene) oxide has been prepared by Brodie by treating the graphite powder with a mixture of potassium chlorate and HNO3 in 1859 (Brodie, 1859). After multiple modifications in the synthesis protocols, in 1957 Hummers and Hoffman developed a simple procedure for the commercial-scale synthesis of graphite oxide, named as the Hummers’ method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). All these discussed processes are under the top-down synthetic approach; GO can also be derived by the bottom-up approach, using the molecular precursors (Tang et al., 2012). Whatever the protocol followed to derive the graphitic oxides, the processes developed a huge number of oxygenated functional groups on the surface. These functional groups play an important role in further modification for biomedical applications. This functionalization can be carried out by following either the covalent or noncovalent methodologies (Dreyer et al., 2010). The presence of oxygen functional groups (–COOH, –CHO, –OH, etc.) acts as the chemical handles that link functional molecules via covalent linkages, in the case of covalent functionalization. Generally, the carboxyl (–COOH) group on the surface of the GO forms an amide linkage with the amine group of proteins or biomolecules. Specifically, the –COOH group reacts with the 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodimide under suitable condition to form an ester, after that the –NH group interact with the ester to form the amide linkage. Sun et al. (2008) prepared polyethylene glycol (PEG) functionalized nanographene oxide (NGO) through the amide linkage formation and observed better dispersion in the physiological environment without any agglomeration. Because of the enhanced photoluminescence behavior in the broad wavelength range (from visible to infrared region), they have been employed as the imaging agent for the in vitro cell imaging. Konkena et al. have prepared the β-aminocyclodextrin functionalized rGO by following this amide linkage formation (Konkena and Vasudevan, 2012). By following this pioneering work, many of the target molecules, such as poly-l-Lysine, DNA, proteins, enzymes, etc. have been attached to the surface of the GO/rGO through the amide linkages. Wang et al. (2011) reported an intracellular protease sensor by conjugating the peptide probe to the GO surface and successfully demonstrated the in vitro quantitative detection of Caspase-3. Moreover, two photon luminescence (TPL) was employed for the cellular imaging and treatment of targeted cancerous cells by using these graphene oxide-based conjugated probes. Li et al. (2012) modified the surface of the graphene oxide nanoparticles (NPs) with transferrin and polyethylene glycol by the covalent functionalization and studied the TPL imaging and targeted therapy.

Furthermore, the existence of the sp2 islands on 2D surface of the GO and rGO facilitates the adsorption of various biomolecules through π–π interactions (noncovalent functionalization) (Georgakilas et al., 2016). The GO/rGO surface is also highly negatively charged and has more affinity to adsorb positively charged molecules through the electrostatic interactions and van der Waals force of attraction (Georgakilas et al., 2016). Chen et al. developed a fluorescence-based biosensor for quantitative detection of concanavalin A, using the maltose grafted aminopyrene (Mal-Apy)-conjugated graphene. It was found that the Mal-Apy conjugated to the graphene surface by the π–π interactions among the pyrene ring of Mal-Apy and graphene surface (Chen et al., 2011b). In another report, a fluorescence-based sensing platform comprised of GO-conjugated aptameric peptide (GO/FITC-IP20) was designed by this noncovalent interaction for the quick detection of protein kinase (Lei et al., 2014). Likewise, a number of graphene-based probes have been developed for the selective and sensitive detection of various bioanalytes, targeted delivery of drugs, and as a biomarker for the in vitro/in vivo cellular imaging (Kim et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2012a; Zhu et al., 2016).

In order to facilitate the internalization of graphene and its derivatives into the cellular membrane and increase their biocompatibility, greater effort has been devoted and small-sized particles called quantum dots been developed. These tiny carbon particles were accidentally discovered by Xu et al. (2004). Quantum dots ideally have a lateral dimension of 10nm and a graphitized sp2 carbon skeleton along with oxo-functionalization at both its surface and edges. As both the sp2 skeleton and oxygen functionalities are available on their surface, both the covalent and noncovalent modifications have been successfully employed for biomedical applications of interest. Very recently, Samantara et al. (2016) have synthesized the CQDs from a molecular precursor, functionalized with an anticancer drug doxorubicin, and demonstrated the pH-triggered in vitro delivery and imaging of the OSCC (oral squamous cell carcinoma) cells. By following both of the top-down and bottom-up synthetic strategies, these CQDs/GQDs were synthesized and employed as the imaging agent for in vitro/in vivo imaging, targeted drug delivery, and designing of various fluorometric sensors for the detection of bioanalytes.

Moreover, the developed oxo-functionalities create defects on the surface of the GO/rGO, making them a suitable platform to accommodate various noble metals or metal oxide nanoparticles (Yin et al., 2015). The addition of the NPs have been carried out either by the ex-situ or in situ synthesis approaches (Gao et al., 2016; Yin et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2016). In the first case, the NPs and rGO/GO are synthesized separately and mixed physically to form the composite material. Whereas in the latter case, the precursors for the NPs and GO are taken together and during the synthesis process, the NPs are grown on the oxygen functionalities of rGO surfaces. Many of the researchers have focused on the preparation of these composites and explore their multi functionalities toward the bioimaging (SERS imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, etc.), sensing, targeted drug delivery, etc. (Kim et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomedical Application of Graphene and Derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: In the last two decades, many of the research personnel have explored the usability of graphene in the development of energy conversion (photovoltaic, fuel cells), energy storage (supercapacitors, batteries), electrochemical sensors, etc. (Choi et al., 2010; Eda and Chhowalla, 2010; Huang et al., 2012). The biomedical application of graphene and its derivatives is an emerging field, to explore their substantial potential. The extremely large surface area, better electrical/thermal conductivity, good mechanical strength, presence of π-electron cloud, ease of functionalization, etc., make graphene and its derivatives suitable to be explored for biomedical applications (Fig. 13.6 ). Therefore, neat graphene and functionalized graphene have already been employed in development of biosensors, gene/drug delivery, bioimaging and in many of the therapeutic treatments, etc. Moreover, the presence of a large number of functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, etc. in the GO allows it to be easily conjugated with a variety of biomolecules (proteins, DNA, enzymes, etc.), imparting its multiple practicalities and modalities in various biomedical applications. Some of the important biomedical applications of graphene and its derivatives are elaborately presented in the following sections.

Sensors are devices that are used to detect/analyze the limit/quantity of a particular material in a specified environment. The main component of a sensor is an active sensing material and a signal transducer. Here, the active sensing material acts as a catalyst that catalyses the biochemical reaction of the bioanalytes and generate the signal. The signal may be electrical, thermal or optical; transmitted by the transducers and then converted to the digital signals. The electrical signal-based biosensors emerges with much more attention due to their selective/sensitive detection of the host material without destroying its environment. Moreover, based on the types of the analytes and construction, the sensors are of two types: (1) chemical sensors, and (2) biosensors. The classification of biosensors has also been carried out based upon the sensing materials; it mainly senses bioanalytes such as proteins, DNA, RNA, nucleotides, tissues, etc. Clark was the first person to develop enzyme electrodes in 1962. After that, so many research opportunities in medical science have been opened (Clark and Lyons, 1962). These enzyme electrodes were used to design the biosensors that are suitable to apply in the field of medicines, biotechnology, agriculture, military/bioterrorism detection, etc. However, the sluggish electron transfer rate of the biomolecules decreases the efficiencies of these electrochemical biosensors. Therefore, the use of additional material in between the active material (enzymes, protein, DNA, etc.) and electrode surface became essential. Many of the nanomaterials, such as metal nanoparticles, alloy NPs, CNTs, carbon nanofibers, etc. have been employed for this purpose. Unfortunately, due to the inconsistencies in the signal amplification, the use of metal/alloy NPs became problematic (Kim et al., 2016). The use of highly conductive CNT was also restricted due to the existence of metallic impurities. After the discovery of graphene in 2004, the new dimensions of electrochemical biosensors have been developed. Moreover, the use of graphene and its derivatives eliminates the limitations associated with the CNTs and metal/alloy based nanomaterial. The unique properties of graphene such as high specific surface area, excellent electrical/thermal conductivity, chemical inertness, ease to functionalize and easy immobilization of the biomolecules, gave it the potential pertinence in the electrochemical biosensors.

Initially, the enzymatic electrochemical sensor generally involves the immobilization of the enzymes onto the electrode surface that was used for the detection of different bioanalytes. In these cases, the electron transfer took place between the active center of the enzyme and the conductive electrode materials. Afterwards, the reagent-less biosensors and various biomedical devices have been designed by immobilizing enzymes onto three-dimensional conducting electrodes. Recently, the mediator, such as graphene and its derivatives are embedded between the conductive electrode and the active enzymes, to relay the direct electron transfer in the electrochemical biosensors. Some of the enzyme-based electrochemical biosensors have been discussed in the following sections.

Diabetes mellitus is a leading cause of death and disability worldwide. It is mainly caused due to the deficiency of insulin in the blood, reflected by the abnormal concentration of glucose in blood (Kim et al., 2016). Therefore, it became essential to monitor the level of glucose in blood by some easy and rapid diagnosis process. Among other enzymes employed for the biosensing of glucose, glucose oxidase (GOx) has been used broadly, due to its higher thermal stability and better catalytic ability for glucose oxidation. The graphene/glucose oxidase-based glucose biosensors also demonstrate highly sensitive, selective and reversibility towards the sensing of glucose. Yang et al. (2010b) have proposed the following reaction mechanisms that happened during the electrochemical analysis with this graphene/GOx based electrode.

In absence of oxygen,

Taking advantage of covalent functionalization, Liu et al. (2010) have developed a GO-based enzymatic sensor for the detection of glucose. The GOx was immobilized on the surface of GO by forming covalent bond between the carboxyl group of the GO with the amine terminal of GOx with the help of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodimide hydrochloride and N-hydroxyl succinimide (NHS). The resulting sensor was found to efficiently detect glucose in a broad range, up to 28mM/mm2, with sensitivity of 8.045mA/cm2 M. Although the GO surface provides numerous oxygen functionalities for conjugation, the limited conductivity restricts in some of the specific applications. Therefore, the reduction of the GO was carried out to form the rGO by various processes, which restores the pi-electron cloud, enhancing the electrical conductivity and also having the oxygen functionalities for conjugation (Si and Samulski, 2008; Park et al., 2009; Shen et al., 2009). For the first time, 3H-phenothiazine-3-one (phenothiazone, PTZ-O) a redox mediator was conjugated to rGO by Ravenna et al. They studied the electron transfer between the flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD)-dependent glucose dehydrogenase and the electrodes. The fabricated electrode demonstrated higher sensitivity of 42mA/M per cm2 with a linear range of glucose detection (0.5–12mM) (Ravenna et al., 2015). In another report, Chen et al. have prepared a graphene-based electrochemiluminescence sensor by encapsulating GOx in between the graphene/Nafion film on glassy carbon electrode surface. They observed a linear response for the glucose detection in the range of 2.0×10−6 to 1.0×10−4 mol/L with robust stability (Chen et al., 2010b). Thereafter, much more effort has been devoted to developing noble metal/graphene-based composites for the selective and more sensitive detection of glucose in physiological environments. In order to avoid an additional step of the electrode modification, Zhang et al. (2017) have employed the electrochemical reduction process of GO directly onto the surface of the glassy carbon electrode, followed by polymerization of l-lysine. The modified electrode shows better electron transfer kinetics and thus employed for the simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA) and uric acid (UA) with detection limits of 2.0, 0.1 and 0.15µM respectively. The electrode has been used to detect these analytes in human urine with recoveries of 104.2% (AA), 95.4% (DA) and 99.9% (UA), respectively and proposed the promising application in physiological and pathological studies.

By following similar electrochemical procedures, Benvidi et al. (2015) have reduced the GO to rGO on a glassy carbon electrode at a particular applied potential (−0.9V for 40s). In order to increase the active sites and enhanced sensitivity of the electrode, the rGO surface had been decorated with Au nanoparticles (by the electrochemical reduction at −0.2V for another 40s). The designed biosensor electrode (GCE/rGO/Au) was used for the detection of the targeted DNA with very low detection limit of 1×10−20 M and also demonstrates the higher selectivity and sensitivity to recognize the complementary and noncomplementary DNA sequences.

Very recently, Ali et al. (2016) have developed a graphene foam (GF)-based immune sensor with femtomolar sensitivity and excellent selectivity for the detection of ErbB2 (an epidermal growth factor receptor). The GF surface was decorated with carbon-doped titanium dioxide nanofiber to increase the accessible surface area of the composite electrode and the sensor was fabricated by covalently conjugating the antibody of ErbB2 by EDC-NHS coupling process (Fig. 13.7 ). It was observed that the porous and higher specific surface area of the sensor enabled it to accommodate more anti-ErbB2 receptors, thus increasing the detection of ErbB2 antigens with very high sensitivity (123.5kΩ/pM for 1.0fM and 43.4kΩ/µM for 0.1pM to 0.1µM).

The fluorescence signal–based biosensor probe works with the principle of FRET between the donor (fluorescence molecule) and acceptor (quencher) molecules. This energy transfer only possible when the excited electronic state of the donor approaches the acceptor at a very short distance (the transfer efficiency is inversely proportional to 106 of the distance between the acceptor and donor) (Wu and Brand, 1994). The advantages of GO, such as (1) hydrophilicity, (2) strong binding affinity to hydrophobic molecules by π–π linkage, (3) capable to link the hydrophilic molecules by hydrogen bonding, (4) better quenching capability, etc. enable to use in many of the fluorescence based sensing strategies (Kim et al., 2010). Many schemes have been formulated to incorporate the GO by combining with the natural nucleic acids, peptides, traditional organic dyes, inorganic particles, etc. in the development of the biosensors. Apiwat et al. have developed an aptasensor by conjugating the fluorescent quencher GO to the Cy5 labeled G8 aptamer. They have demonstrated this conjugated sensing platform for the sensitive detection of glycated human serum albumin in diabetes mellitus with a limit of detection of 50µg/mL (Apiwat et al., 2016). In this regard, many of the efforts have been made to develop fluorescent dye–based biosensors, but the high cost and lower photostability of these dyes limits their application and creates motivation to develop alternative materials. Conversely, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) proved to be a potent alternative to these traditional dyes. The fascinating properties of CDs, such as their ease of preparation, nonblinking properties and better photostability, etc. make them a suitable material for the designing of the fluorescent biosensors (Yang et al., 2013c). Cui et al. (2015) prepared a fluorescent biosensor using the oligodeoxyribonucleotide labeled CDs and GO. The sensor shows a very sensitive and selective detection of the Hg2+, such as heavy metal ions with very lower limits of detection of 2.6nM. In many of the strategies, the bare CDs are also employed as the sensing platform for the detection of biomolecules and hazardous heavy metal ions.

Bioimaging is one of the important biotechnological tools for development of biomedical and biological sciences through the novel methods of imaging of specific molecular pathways in living cells and disease processes (Zhu et al., 2016). In addition, the monitoring and visualization of molecules in the living cell is a key parameter to acquire in-depth knowledge in cell biology and biomedical sciences (Stender et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2016). The transparency to visible light and narrow size of the individual molecules makes it impossible to observe the compartments and molecules inside the cell. Accordingly, the development of efficient and advanced probes became an important task for the researchers. After many of the trials, the combination of graphene and imaging science approaches emerged for the generation of graphene based probes for the bioimaging systems. As discussed in the previous sections, the unique optical properties, ease of cellular uptake, large surface area, tunable chemical modification, better fluorescence quenching capacity and Raman activeness of graphene and its derivatives make them a suitable material for the bioimaging systems (Schedin et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2013). Moreover, functionalized graphene possesses a huge number of functionalities that can hold a variety of recognition molecules for better imaging performance. Although the graphene derivatives perform as better fluorescence material in broad wavelength ranges, because of their quenching ability they play a promising role in the platform of bioimaging technology. The existing sp2 domains in graphene and its derivatives quench the luminescence properties of the nearby fluorescent dyes, quantum dots, metal clusters, etc. by means of the FRET mechanism (Dong et al., 2010; Kasry et al., 2012). It has been documented that the graphene oxide shows less quenching efficiency, compared to graphene, but it increases in the case of the rGO (due to restoration of the π-electron cloud).

The fluorescence imaging is a noninvasive tool that uses the photons that are emitted from the fluorescent probes and is able to create images of cells, molecules, tissues and different organs of a body (Baker and Baker, 2010; Janib et al., 2010). Different research groups have adopted various methods to modify the graphene surface for use as the bio marker. Sun et al. (2008) prepared a PEG conjugated nano-GO composite (nGO-PEG) for the first time by covalent linking, followed by modification with an antibody Rituxan (antiCD20). Although the probe (nGO-PEG-Rituxan) has been demonstrated successfully for the targeted fluorescent imaging of Raji B-cells, the lower quantum yield limits the in vivo imaging application (Sun et al., 2008). Afterwards, for higher luminescence quantum yield, many of the groups have prepared the fluorescent dye conjugated GO/rGO hybrids for the bioimaging purpose. In 2010, for the first time, Yang et al. studied the in vivo behavior of nanographene sheets (NGS) in animal models (Fig. 13.9 ). A fluorescent dye Cy7 was conjugated to the PEG-functionalized NGS. It demonstrated the in vivo fluorescence imaging and efficient near infrared photothermal therapy of a tumor xenografted mice (Yang et al., 2010a). Besides the fluorescent dyes, the photosensitizers have also been used by many of the research groups for the graphene surface modification for the imaging study. However, in some of the instances the fluorescence property was observed to be quenched because of the strong π–π stacking interaction between the porphyrin rings of the dye/sensitizer with the plane of GO/graphene. Although this quenching effect restricts some of the imaging applications, by employing this concept, many of the fluorescent-based sensing platforms have been developed for qualitative/quantitative detection of various metal ions and molecules. With the advantage of quenching property of graphene, Mao et al. synthesized 1,2-bis-(2-pyren-1-ylmethylamino-ethoxy) ethane (NPEY) conjugated graphene nanosheets for in vitro fluorometric detection of the Mn2+ ions. It was observed that the fluorescence behavior of NPEY is quenched after forming the π–π linkage with the graphene surface, due to the photo-induced electron transfer process. However, in the presence of Mn2+, the π–π linkage is ruptured, forming a Mn-NPEY complex retaining the fluorescence property, leading to an efficient sensing platform (Mao et al., 2013b).

Because of the potential photobleaching of these fluorescent dyes and sensitizers, other luminescent materials like, quantum dots (CQDs, GQDs, ZnS QDs, etc.), metal clusters and many of the up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have been used to conjugate the GO/rGO surfaces to get efficient fluorescent imaging. Wang et al. reported a multifunctional nanoplatform by covalently grafting the UCNPs to the PEG-functionalized NGO surface followed by loading the phthalocyanine (ZnPc). The composite (UCNPs/NGO/ZnPc) were found to be act as not only a potential up-conversion luminescent imaging agent but also has been used for the photodynamic and photothermal therapy demonstrating higher therapeutic efficiency for the cancer treatment (Wang et al., 2013). In another report, Hu et al. (2012) prepared a multifunctional QDs/rGO composite and demonstrated the in vivo/in vitro imaging and photothermal therapy. Before the composite preparation, the QDs surface were functionalized with tri-n-octylphosphine oxide (TOPO) and 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid to form a monolayer organic shell that acts as a spacer between the QDs and rGO surface restricting the fluorescent quenching effect of rGO. Likewise many of the QDs/graphene composite have been derived and their bioimaging performance have been explored. Conversely, the low photobleaching, negligible cytotoxicity, ease of preparation and environmental friendliness certify the carbon/graphene quantum dots as the better candidature as an imaging agent over these transition metal based QDs and fluorescent dyes. A biocompatible, two photon fluorescent N-doped GQDs was prepared by Liu et al. following a solvothermal method (Liu et al., 2013a). These N-GQDs performed as a potential imaging material for the cellular and deep tissue imaging (with an imaging depth of 1800µm) opening many opportunities for biomedical applications. In another report, Pan et al. (2015) reported microwave assisted synthesis of fluorescent CQDs by heating citric acid in formaldehyde and employed as a multicolor bioimaging agent for the labeling of MCF-7 (human breast cancer cell). Very recently, our group have developed the N, S codoped CQDs from molecular precursors such as the Good’s buffer molecules by following a facile single step hydrothermal reduction method. In this case, no further surface modification and functionalization was carried out and the doped hetero atoms (N and S) came from the starting precursors. The bare CQDs shows good photoluminescence quantum yield with better cell viability and demonstrated as a suitable imaging agent for in vitro imaging of the H357 cells (Samantara et al., 2016).

Among different vibrational spectroscopic techniques, the Raman imaging is a powerful tool having higher signal-noise ratio, negligible photobleaching and capable to solve chemical/biochemical shortcomings in a nondestructive and nonperturbing manner (Lin et al., 2016). it is an inelastic scattering of phonons obtained from the molecular vibrational excitation modes which are completely different from that in the case of phonon absorption and emission in the fluorescence spectroscopic analysis (Yoo et al., 2015). The graphene and its derivatives are found to be highly Raman active showing two distinct bands (G-band at 1580cm−1 and D-band at 1350cm−1). Furthermore, this activity can be enhanced by making composites with the noble metal nanoparticles (Kim et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2012, 2013b). This may be due to both the surface plasmon resonance and the dipole effect of metal nanoparticles. Ma et al. prepared a NGO-wrapped AuNPs composite and demonstrated it as an efficient Raman imaging probe and drug delivery system. It was observed that both the D- and G-bands increased in one order magnitude, in comparison to the NGO sheets, resulting the sensitive imaging of the internalized Au@NGOs in the HeLa cells (Ma et al., 2013). Likewise, many synthetic strategies have been followed and many of the methods (like in-situ and ex-situ synthetic methods) have been developed to conjugate noble metal nanoparticles onto the GO/rGO surfaces and explore their performance for in vitro Raman imaging. Song et al. developed alkyne-PEG functionalized AgCu@graphene (ACG) nanoparticles by using the CVD method (Song et al., 2014). In comparison to the alkyne PEG, the ACG showed an enhanced Raman signal and thus employed for the cell labeling, Raman imaging of MCF-7 cells and SERS detection. They utilized both the D, G and alkyne modes for the colocalization of the ACGs demonstrating better intracellular localization capability (Fig. 13.10 ).

Like the fluorometric and Raman imaging techniques, magnetic resonance imaging is also a noninvasive imaging tool broadly used to image the anatomy and function of the tissues quantitatively with excellent resolution. Although this technique has been used broadly for biomedical diagnosis, it suffers from long signal acquisition time and low sensitivity (Janib et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2016). Since the longitudinal (T1) and transverse (T2) relaxation times of water molecule paramagnetic moment are environment-dependent, various MRI contrast agents have been developed. In this regard, the Gd3+ based agents (for T1) and iron oxide nanoparticle (IONPs) (for T2) are normally employed to get the brighter and darker images respectively. However, the metal ions of Gd and Mn show toxicity, due to their nonselective coordination of the biomolecules (Caravan et al., 1999). Many of the research groups have taken advantage of the oxygenated functional groups of the GO and rGO to mitigate the toxic effects of these contrasting agents. Gizzatov et al. prepared a Gd3+ ion-conjugated graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) for high performance contrasting agent for MRI application. They observed an enhanced relaxation with 16 (for r1) and 21 (for r2) times more than the clinically available Gd-based contrasting agent for both the T1 and T2 MRI applications (Gizzatov et al., 2014). Conversely, the superparamagnetic hybrid of IONPs) with GNR has been explored as an T2 MRI contrasting agent (Yang et al., 2013a). Likewise, the bare IONP/graphene composite materials and the ligand-coated IONP also have been successfully anchored to the GO surface to achieve a more contrasting MRI agent (Chen et al., 2011c). Apart from the use of paramagnetic metal nanoparticles and their complexes, the doped graphene oxide was used for MRI. For the first time, Hu et al. reported in 2013 the magnetic metal-free fluorinated graphene oxide and studied the MRI contrasting performance (Fig. 13.11 ). Here, the dipole in the C-F bond acted like a paramagnetic center, thus performing as a contrasting agent (Hu, 2014).

Among different carbon materials, graphene has been chosen as the dynamic platform for the attachment and targeted delivery of various drugs, but the insolubility in the physiological environment hinders the practical application (Shen et al., 2012a). Conversely, GO (an important derivative of graphene) is found to be more suitable for the biomedical applications because of its significant properties, such as large surface area, better biocompatibility, solubility in physiological conditions, existence of various oxygenated functional groups, etc. (Shan et al., 2009). The functional groups, mainly –COOH and –OH of GO facilitate the conjugation of drug molecules, nanoparticles, DNAs, quantum dots, and other biomolecules, lending GO toward the multifunctional performance in diverse biomedical applications. Unfortunately, GO tends to aggregate when it interacts with the salts or proteins of cell culture media or serum present in the biological buffered solutions (Liu et al., 2008; Sun et al., 2008). Therefore, several approaches have been adopted and the GO surface has been functionalized, as discussed in the previous section. By covalent functionalization process, molecules such as PEG, sulfonic acid, chitosan, and folic acid, etc. are attached to the GO surface (Depan et al., 2011; Feng et al., 2011; Rana et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2010). This functionalized GO shows better biocompatibility with enhanced aqueous solubility and stability in cell culture media, phosphate buffered solution, serum, etc. This also produces a promising platform for the targeted delivery of water insoluble anticancer drugs (like doxorubicin (DOX)) and various antiinflammatory drugs. Liu et al. (2008) covalently modified the GO surface with PEG by carbodimide catalyzed method to achieve the highly dispersible GO, instead of aggregations. This GO-PEG was found to form complex with SN-38 (a potent topoisomerase I inhibitor) rapidly by simply mixing the SN-38 (in DMSO) with the GO-PEG aqueous solution. They observed that drug-loaded GO-PEG performs as a potent nanomaterial to kill the human colon cancer cell line HCT-116, which was equally effective as the free SN-38 and around 1000 times more effective than the CPT-116. Likewise, the polyethylenimine (PEI) has been conjugated to the GO surface via the carbodimide linking mechanism to form a biocompatible GO-PEI composite. The GO-PEI was demonstrated to be a potential carrier for the targeted delivery of the short interfering RNA and various anticancer drugs (Zhang et al., 2011a). Zhang et al. (2016) developed a graphene nanocarrier for the in vivo targeted drug delivery. For the first time, they conjugated the NGO-PEG with the octreotide (OCT, a bio target molecule) to form NGO-PEG-OCT composite (Fig. 13.12 ). Owing to better targeting efficacy and loading capability, the anticancer drug DOX was loaded and the workers observed a remarkably higher cellular intake, facilitating the targeted delivery system. Moreover, the near infrared absorption made the composite more potent for the photothermal ablation of the tumors. In another report, Alibolandi et al. (2017) prepared dextran-conjugated NGO sheets (GO-DEX) followed by linking with a single stranded DNA (ssDNA) aptamer. The composite showed higher biocompatibility toward the 4T1 mammary carcinoma cell line and was subsequently loaded with curcumin (CUR, having an antineoplastic effect), that easily binds to the surface of GO-DEX-ssDNA by π–π interaction (GO-DEX-ssDNA-Apt-CUR). The GO-DEX-ssDNA-Apt-CUR composite demonstrated to be more toxic toward the 4T1 and MCF-7 cell line, suggesting a potential application in the cancer therapeutic treatment. The in vitro treatment of the CQDs/DOX with HEK-273 cell lines demonstrated to be an efficient material having higher toxicity.

Like the drug delivery systems, gene therapy is also a promising therapeutic alternative for treatment of various diseases such as Parkinson’s disease, cancer, and cystic fibrosis, etc. (Shen et al., 2012a). It requires a gene vector to defend the DNA from nuclease degradation, enabling the cellular uptake with higher transfection ability. Accordingly, many of the researchers have taken the challenge to develop better gene vectors having higher transfection capability. Liu and Chen et al. studied the gene delivery using the PEI functionalized GO (PEI-GO) (Chen et al., 2011a; Kovtyukhova and Mallouk, 2011). The positively charged GO-PEI surface was found to electrostatically bind the plasmid DNA (pDNA) onto its surface and demonstrated better transfection efficiency in comparison to the bare PEI. The observation suggested the promising candidature of the GO-PEI for the gene delivery systems. Bao et al. reported GO surface-modification with chitosan and conjugation with pDNA. The formed composite material exhibited a reasonable transfection efficiency in the HeLa cells (Bao et al., 2011). Very recently, Hsieh et al. demonstrated conjugation of PEI modified rGO surface with a neurospecific peptide neurotensin (NT) and pDNA. Under the NIR, more pDNA was released, compared to the normal release process. This combined effect of neurospecific targeting peptide and irradiation of NIR laser triggered the efficacy of the nanoplatform for gene therapeutic treatment in central nervous system (Hsieh et al., 2016).

It is a multidisciplinary field that utilizes the knowledge of medicine, engineering, and biology to develop the biomimetic tissues (biomaterials) for organ transplantation, therapeutics and diagnosis research works (Langer and Vacanti, 2017; Shin et al., 2016; Webber et al., 2015). These biomaterials are the essential factors of tissue engineering, due to their significant role in functioning of cells, and to modulate the cell–cell interactions. Since different tissues in the body have different electrical, mechanical, and physical properties, so a single material may not clone the properties of a particular tissue. Much more effort has been paid to develop composite/hybrid materials for the manufacturing of artificial tissues. The excellent electrical conductivity and extraordinary mechanical strength of graphene/graphene derivatives own the crown of suitability in tissue engineering (Schinwald et al., 2012; Shin et al., 2013). The researchers have prepared a variety of composite of graphene with different bioactive materials for the designing of biomaterials of craved characteristics. Generally, wet/dry spinning, 3D printing, coating and blending of hydrogels processes have been followed for the development of graphene based biomaterials (Cha et al., 2014; Shen et al., 2012b; Zhang et al., 2011b). In some instances, it has been found that the graphene and graphene derivative materials have been conjugated to other biomaterials to increase physical, mechanical and electrical properties in the tissue engineering. Since most of the materials used in tissue engineering processes are insulating in nature, thus graphene have been incorporated for the better electrically conductive scaffolds. By using a different concentration of GO, Shin et al. (2013) were able to tune the mechanical strength of a GO-based hydrogel in a range of 8–24kPa. Several groups have used the composites of polymers with the graphene-based derivatives to prepare printable inks. The addition of graphene derivatives to the polymer matrix increases the shear thinning behavior and also enhanced the electrical conductivity of the printed polymer ink (Liu et al., 2015). Zhang et al. (2011b) incorporated GO into the poly(vinyl alcohol) matrix to strengthen the hydrogels. In order to develop the surface properties of the biomaterials (topography, surface stiffness, etc.), Kalbacova et al. (2010) prepared graphene-coated SiO2. They observed a triggered stretching and growth of human mesenchymal stem cells. Likewise, many of the strategies have been formulated to employ graphene and derivatives as a potential agent to fabricate composite materials in tissue engineering, opening opportunities to explore more biomedical application.

More work on the antibacterial activity of single-walled and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT/MWCNT) has been carried out, but their high cost and requirement of sophisticated instruments for synthesis demands to develop an alternative material (Kang et al., 2007, 2008). After the isolation of graphene sheets from the bulk graphite in 2004, many of its inherent properties came into the screen. Therefore, graphene and its derivatives have been employed for many of the applications in the field of biomedicine and therapeutics. The extremely high surface aspect ratio (ratio of lateral size to atomic thickness) and higher surface area provides an ideal and promising platform for microorganism interactions, leading to disinfection and killing. Out of different proposed toxicity mechanism (oxidative stress, rupture of cell membranes, and cutting off intracellular metabolic routes), oxidative stress was found to be the most acceptable one to present the toxicity of graphene-based materials to bacteria such as single celled microorganisms (Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2010; Kang et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2011; Nel et al., 2006). Although not enough work has been done on the antibacterial activity of graphene-based materials, some distinguished works are there, presenting interesting antimicrobial activity of graphene-based materials. Liu et al. (2011) have analyzed the antibacterial activity of graphene and its derivatives (like graphite, graphite oxide, graphene oxide and rGO) by treating them with the E. coli bacteria. The comparison study revealed highest antibacterial activity of the GO among the other derivatives. A contradictory report was presented by Ruiz et al. (2011) showing a faster growth of bacteria in a culture media after addition of GO. Likewise, the rGO nanosheets, the rGO nanowalls showed more antibacterial activity toward both the gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and gram-negative (E. coli) bacterial cells (Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2010). It has been proposed that the bacterial cell-killing ability was due to the better charge transfer between the bacteria and sharp edges of the reduced GO nanowalls, compared to the nonreduced form during the contact interaction.

Conversely, the graphene and graphene derivatives perform the role of antibacterial agent well, by forming composites with noble metal and metal oxide nanoparticles. Based on the historical backgrounds and present study, the nanosized gold (Au), zinc oxide (ZnO), titanium dioxide (TiO2), etc. have antimicrobial activity, due to their small size and higher surface-to-volume ratio but don’t have significant antibacterial activity. Whereas, silver (Ag) and copper (Cu) nanoparticles show better antibacterial activities (Perdikaki et al., 2016). Xu et al. proposed a mechanism on the antibacterial effect of Ag nanoparticles. According to them, during the treatment with bacterial cells, the AgNPs strongly interact with the thiol (present in the cell membrane proteins) and phosphorous groups (of the cytoplasm), which negatively affect the cellular respiration and cell division, leading to cell death (Xu et al., 2011). Although, many researchers have developed various shaped and sized Ag NPs and explored their antibacterial activities, their strong affinity to form aggregates reduces the activity. Therefore, the graphene-based 2D materials have been employed to form composites with NPs to achieve better antibacterial performance, due to the (1) combined effect of metal NPs and graphene, and (2) reducing the aggregation of the metal NPs. By following a single/rapid synthetic process and using p-hexasulfonic acid calix [6] arene (SCX6) as particle stabilizer, Kellici et al. prepared the Ag/graphene nanocomposite (Ag-rGO). The Ag-rGO showed excellent antibacterial properties against both the gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria (Kellici et al., 2016). Perdikaki et al. prepared both the monometallic (Ag, Cu nanoparticles) and bimetallic (Ag-Cu) NPs-based graphene composite by the CVD method (Perdikaki et al., 2016). It was observed that, in comparison to the mono metallic composites, the Ag-Cu/graphene composite demonstrated significant suppression of the bacterial growth.

In this chapter, we have discussed the graphene-based materials, starting from their historical backgrounds to their application in various biomedical fields. Although graphene has inherent properties such as extremely high electrical conductivity, large theoretical surface area, excellent optical/thermal properties, its hydrophobic nature in the aqueous medium restricts some of the distinct biomedical applications. Therefore, an emphasis has been given on the synthesis of its important derivative i.e., graphene oxide, and then a brief discussion on various ways to functionalize graphene and its derivatives has been presented. Owing to their ability to accommodate a variety of molecules/probes by both the covalent and noncovalent types of linking, higher biocompatibility, and better solubility in the physiological condition, it has been employed in diverse biomedical applications, ranging from the bioimaging to the targeted delivery of various drugs. Although much more work has already been done there are a lot of challenges to be taken care before the final naturalistic development of graphene. These include: (1) synthesis of high quality graphene in commercial scale, (2) to design ecofriendly synthetic protocols, (3) synthesis of single graphene in large scale in a cost effective manner, (4) a systematic synthetic route to develop the graphene based hybrid materials, (5) to develop a controlled way of selective functionalization of the graphene surface, and (6) to explore the fundamental principles involved in the interaction between the graphene surface and probes, etc. Furthermore, a multidisciplinary approach is highly indispensable to explore the profound performance of this miracle material, graphene, in the near future.
